name: Ft Randall
description_short: Fort Randall is a historic military outpost ruin located on the Missouri River near the South Dakota-Nebraska border, now managed by the National Park Service as part of the Missouri National Recreational River.
description_long: Fort Randall was established in June 1856 by General William S. Harney as a military post strategically located on the west bank of the Missouri River. Named after Colonel Daniel Randall, the Deputy Paymaster General of the U.S. Army, the fort served as a crucial outpost for over three decades. Its primary mission was to maintain peace between American Indians and white settlers, as well as among Native American tribes themselves. The post provided troops and routed supplies for various government operations in the region during a transformative period in American frontier history. Fort Randall served longer as a continuously occupied military post than any other fort on the upper Missouri River, operating from 1856 until its abandonment on October 31, 1892. The fort is notable for housing famous historical figures including Buffalo Bill Cody, mountain man Jim Bridger, and Civil War General Phillip Sheridan. Most significantly, it held Sitting Bull and his band of Hunkpapa Sioux from July 1881 to November 1883 under loose surveillance following the Battle of Little Big Horn. The post also briefly imprisoned Chief Standing Bear and his brother Big Snake in 1877 in retaliation for their resistance to tribal relocation. Fort Randall is distinguished by its architectural contribution—a cross-shaped chapel, library, and lodge building erected in 1875 that became the fort's most conspicuous improvement and cost approximately $20,000 to construct. Today, Fort Randall exists as an archaeological site managed by the National Park Service within the Missouri National Recreational River, offering visitors educational value through its historical significance and preserved remains. The site provides important insights into frontier military operations, Native American history, and the complex interactions between federal forces and tribal nations during the 19th century.
